pull.title: guix: Split manifest into build and codesign manifests
pull.body: This PR narrows the scope of the Guix environments to include only the minimum dependencies required for specific tasks, namely building and codesigning.

Rebased on top of the merged https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/34944.

~0 on the second commit, until we have an idea how the split builds will actually work. i.e different manifests will mean having to rebuild depends between compilations? I don't think there's a need to refactor code for no benefit, at this point.

> ~0 on the second commit, until we have an idea how the split builds will actually work

These two commits were split from my WIP branch with the entire solution, which I'll submit as draft PR shortly.

> ... i.e different manifests will mean having to rebuild depends between compilations?

Yes, because of a different `GUIX_ENVIRONMENT` value.

> which I'll submit as draft PR shortly.

I don't think any more changes should be made, without seeing the final branch.

> Yes, because of a different GUIX_ENVIRONMENT value.

Yes, and I don't think having to build depends twice, is good.

> > Yes, because of a different GUIX_ENVIRONMENT value.
> 
> Yes, and I don't think having to build depends twice, is good.

This has been addressed in this [commit](https://github.com/hebasto/bitcoin/commit/d3f57d8816b4dad1cbcdb1fd47a6efaf708c4ba4) from my WIP branch.

comments.body:
As noted in the `oscrypto` [documentation](https://github.com/wbond/oscrypto/blob/master/readme.md), it dynamically loads OpenSSL on Linux:
> ... `ctypes.util.find_library()` is used to located OpenSSL. Alpine Linux does not have an appropriate install by default for `find_library()` to work properly. Instead, `oscrypto.use_openssl()` must be called with the path to the OpenSSL shared libraries.

This exact reasoning applies to Guix. Because `find_library()` won't work reliably here, packages consuming `python-oscrypto` must explicitly call `oscrypto.use_openssl()` with the correct library path. This is exactly what the new patch implements for `signapple`.

@achow101, as the author of `signapple`, might be able to weigh in on this as well.

> then that should be fixed upstream (i.e how does it work for a Python only consumer, with no compiler install

My understanding is that it generally doesn't as it implements no actual cryptography in Python.

The library is named oscrypto because it uses things generally available in the operating system. Platforms like Windows and MacOS provide cryptography libraries by default. For Linux, it uses OpenSSL or LibreSSL as these are often installed on operating systems. I think this requirement is assumed to be safe because Python itself has default enabled optional modules that require OpenSSL (ssl and hashlib modules), so if Python is available, OpenSSL is very likely also available.
